items.body:
In providers/applications that create custom libctx'es via OSSL_LIB_CTX_new, its possible, if the default provider has never been initaialized during the lifetime of the linked libcrypto, that we actually wind up creating the default libctx when we free the aforementioned custom libctx via, as an example:
```
legacy_teardown->
 OSSL_LIB_CTX_free->
  ossl_lib_ctx_is_default->
   get_default_context->
    get_thread_default_context->
     default_context_do_init
```
While this isn't catastrophic, its needless, and in some cases has the potential to leak memory (for instance if a provider is loaded and unloaded repeatedly in an environment in which the provider is linked to libcrypto.so while the calling application is statically linked to libcrypto.a

Its also fairly easy to clean up, by adding an internal parameter to gate the creation of the default libctx on the request of the caller, so do that here

Fixes openssl/project#1846

In the [providers] section it is now possible to inject an override of both the OPENSSL_MODULES and underlying built-in MODULESDIR default that applies to the immediately following providers, until another such override is added.

Because section name/value pairs are deduplicated by name, to apply multiple settings an option "<whatever>." unique prefix is supported.

    [providers]
    modulesdir = /some/where1
    providerA = ...
    providerB = ...
    1.modulesdir = /some/where2
    providerC = ...
    2.modulesdir =
    providerD =

Setting the value empty clears the override for the subsequent providers, until perhaps another override is added...

## Summary

Users reading the documentation for the `-<cipher>` option often misunderstand
the syntax. The notation `B<-I<cipher>>` renders as "-cipher" with "cipher" in
italics, leading users to think they should type `-cipher aes-128-cbc` when the
correct usage is `-aes-128-cbc` (the cipher name directly as the option).

See: https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/787468/

This PR updates the documentation in:
- **openssl-genpkey**: Added explicit clarification and multiple examples
- **openssl-enc**: Added clarification about the option syntax
- **openssl-pkey**: Added clarification and fixed a typo ("and and" → "and")

All three now:
- Explicitly state that the cipher name is prepended with a hyphen
- Clarify that it's NOT an argument to a `-cipher` flag
- Reference `openssl list -cipher-algorithms` to discover available ciphers

Fixes #26089

## Summary
- Fix incorrect threading documentation in `BIO_s_dgram_pair(3)` man page
- Clarify that only `BIO_recvmmsg` and `BIO_sendmmsg` support simultaneous use by multiple threads
- Document that other BIO calls like `BIO_read`, `BIO_write`, etc. are not safe for concurrent use due to the BIO API storing state (such as retry flags) on the BIO object itself

As we are already adding lists to verify param, as discussed, let's just separate out
the lists of things we wish to validate against the certificate cn in the subject

As a result X509_verify_cert no longer will care about the ALWAYS_CHECK_SUBJECT
or NEVER_CHECK_SUBJECT flags - you simply set what you want to do before calling it
by adding whichever names you want to check against the SAN DNSnames, and whichever
names you want to check against the CN 

The legacy X509_check_host and friends still do respect the flags, but as we have
not reccomended their use for ages, let's deprecate them.

Fixes: https://github.com/openssl/project/issues/1897

items.body: Add software prefetch to the AVX2 base64 encoder hot loop, hiding L2 cache latency on large buffers. Reorder the per-vector pipeline so each vector completes its full sequence (load, shuffle, bit-split, lookup) before the next begins, rather than batching all loads then all shuffles. This gives the out-of-order engine independent work chains across execution ports. Together the two changes improve average throughput by ~19% and best-case by ~7% on a 1 MB buffer (1000 runs). These KDE charts are taken over 1000 executions on a Intel(R) Core(TM) Ultra 7 165H. The plot shows the distribution tightening around the peak: baseline had a wide spread from 4,000–12,000 MB/s while the optimized version clusters above 9,500 MB/s.

* Use EVP\_\*\_CTX_set_params instead of legacy EVP\_\*\_CTX_ctrl. The next
  controls have been replaced with params:
  - EVP_PKEY_CTRL_SET_IV to OSSL_EXCHANGE_PARAM_KDF_UKM
  - EVP_PKEY_CTRL_CIPHER to OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_CIPHER
  - EVP_MD_CTRL_TLSTREE to OSSL_DIGEST_PARAM_TLSTREE
  - EVP_CTRL_TLSTREE to OSSL_CIPHER_PARAM_TLSTREE
* Use provider interface during TLS setup and routines
* Use EVP_PKEY_get0_type_name instead of legacy EVP_PKEY_get_id
* Don't translate EVP_PKEY_CTRL_PEER_KEY to params. Key exchange algorithms
  use this control with static keys which can't be achieved with encrypt
  context (only derive context supports setting peer key). This therefore
  results in the inability to use the next ciphersuites in some cases (see
  draft-chudov-cryptopro-cptls-04) with provider-implemented PKEYs:
  - TLS_GOSTR341094_WITH_28147_CNT_IMIT
  - TLS_GOSTR341001_WITH_28147_CNT_IMIT
  - TLS_GOSTR341094_WITH_NULL_GOSTR3411
  - TLS_GOSTR341001_WITH_NULL_GOSTR3411

items.body:
Recently, curl dropped support for the openssl quic stack, which led to failures in our interop testing, as our quic interop tests relied on using curl with openssls quic stack to do http3 tests.

We put in an interim solution to fix this, by backing off our curl build in our test docker file to build curl at a commit level prior to the removal of that support.

This is the longer term solution.  If curl doesn't support the openssl quic stack, its no longer useful to us, so lets replace its use in our quic interop container to use our custom http3 client from demos/http3 instead.

This PR does 2 things:

1) Updates the ossl-http3-demo.c client to support:
   a) making multiple requests to a server (instead of the static single request to fetch "/" that was there before
   b) adds an option to specify a download directory to save the requested files to

items.body:
As far as I understand this code is unmaintained.

Many codepaths implemented are long obsolete, buggy, or had recent-ish
cryptoanalysis results showing it is insecure.

Also, many newer features and algorithms are not implemented for up to
date ciphers, modes, and extensions.

The code as is, does not test against any known alternative certified
implementations. At the same time, most popular consumer facing
website supports IETF/NIST prime256r1 curve now. Hence likely usage is
restricted to website that require a certified implementation instead
(such as CryptoPro).

Desite the NO_GOST ifdefs, actually many gost-specific code-paths are
not guarded with such ifdefs and have leaked into the shared libraries
ABI and in active libcrypto/libssl hotpaths, despite being effectively
no-op for the NO_GOST builds.

The useful portions of this code, that are still relevant and in use
could be reinstante, assuming there is a non-geoblocked TLS server
with which connectivity can be tested that is running uptodate
alternative implementation (for example CryptoPro); and that all
codepaths are correctly guarded with configuration features and
ifdefs, such as to not affect the abi of the NO_GOST builds. At
parity, with forexample NO_FIPS builds.

Potentially, design would be interesting to expose TLS extensions via
the provider, such that provider could do partial processing of the
certificate chain validation; and TLS extensions handling, such that
one could maintain all of these relevant pieces completely in the
provider.

cc @mattcaswell

ps. @reptiloidla, there is likely a mid-air collision as I have been
working on this PR for the past few days. Note, it still seems to be
true that many of the things removed in this PR are not worth keeping
for up to date support of these algorithms.

Also note:
- https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29921
- https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29303

So we have obsolete code in mainline; and some outstanding fixes & new features being proposed; and this proposal to remove all of this - as many codepaths are obsolete.

items.body:
our fuzzer caught this:
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/actions/runs/21625445341/job/62324333796

Overnight.

We're getting a heap buffer overrun in the SRTP KDF.

Its caused by the fact that the fuzzer will occasionally generate salt parameters that are very small, which passes the
OSSL_PARAM_get_octet_string function, but isn't long enough to be a valid salt.  Because of this, when we actually do the key derivation, the SRTPKDF function assumes the salt is long enough and blindly attempts to copy KDF_SRTP_SALT_LEN (14) bytes from the fetched parameter into a local buffer, resulting in an overrun.

items.title: Fix buffer overrun in SRTPKDF

items.body:
Fixes issue in Discussion 22861

Running the commandline with "openssl enc -id-aes256-wrap-pad ...." does not work for large private keys (keys are larger for PQ). This operation uses the cipher BIO (bio_enc) which has a fixed size buffer. Note that the encrypt was not failing but the decrypt operation did fail.
Investigating this issue uncovered the following issues:

1) AES_WRAP_PAD is documented as only working for non streaming cases
   (since all of the input is required). This meant that although
   encrypt did not fail, it could never pass on a subsequent decrypt.
   A flag has been added to track the update() and fail if it is called
   multiple times.

2) An internal function returned an int which could be negative and it
   was assigned to a size_t which ignored the error condition.

The size of the bio_enc buffer has been changed from 4K to 8K.

items.body:
Currently the state machine moves the read record layer to the next epoch base off of state events. The problem with this is when either the client or the server sent out a record during the handshake that causes an alert. If the state machine moved either the client or server to the next epoch it will not be able to handle that alert message. This approach delays moving the read record layer to the next epoch until we actually receive a record in that epoch. This allows us to process the alert messages.

Fixes: openssl/project#1786
Fixes: openssl/project#1785
Fixes: openssl/project#1759

items.body:
We expect a custom legacy method (e.g. RSA_METHOD) to work even if you have set if via RSA_set_method() on an RSA object that you assign to an EVP_PKEY. Such an object is imported into the default provider, and it should retain the
custom RSA_METHOD during that process. The same applies for other similar methods (DSA_METHOD, EC_KEY_METHOD, DH_METHOD).

In order for this to work we have to break the normal rules about passing complex objects across the provider boundary. But since this is just for the default provider we can get away with it.

This is an alternative implementation to that shown in #29942. The difference here is that we pass the whole low level object across to the provider, rather than just the METHOD. This has the advantage then any associated ex_data is retained. Another difference is that I am passing the object across via a thread local variable rather than a param. This is because I need to do this in the "newdata" call - but that does not accept parameters, and I don't want to amend the provider API to support this.

Fixes https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29702
